title: BioVeil MATRIX: Uncovering and categorizing vulnerabilities of agentic biological AI scientists
summary: Agentic AI scientists equipped with domain-specific tools are rapidly entering scientific workflows across disciplines, with especially strong uptake in the life sciences where they can be used for literature synthesis, sequence analysis, and experimental planning support. While these systems accelerate biological research, they also introduce risks for dual-use applications that are not captured by current model-centric safety evaluations. We present evidence that current agentic AI scientists, including Biomni and K-Dense, are willing to assist with dual-use tasks that are blocked by base model safeguards. We also found that in a paired evaluation framework for biology and chemistry prompts involving Weapons of Mass Destruction proxies (WMDP), agentic scaffolding of Biomni increased the benchmark performance relative to the underlying standalone model, producing measurable capability uplift. We believe it is necessary to include additional safeguards in existing models and build future tools from the ground up with agentic vulnerabilities in mind. To systematically categorize broader risks, we introduce BioVeil MATRIX, a defensive taxonomy that maps AI-enabled biosecurity risks using 10 tactical categories (TA01--TA10) and 22 different techniques. We propose to use this taxonomy as a baseline for future AI scientist development and generate specialized benchmarks and protocols for red-teaming these vulnerabilities before public deployment. BioVeil MATRIX can be found at: https://bioveilmatrix.com/

AI.tldr: Agentic AI scientists in biology are willing to assist with dual-use tasks and show capability uplift on WMD-related benchmarks despite base model safeguards, motivating a new defensive taxonomy (BioVeil MATRIX) for categorizing and red-teaming biosecurity risks.
AI.motivation: Current model-centric safety evaluations fail to capture the dual-use risks introduced by agentic AI scientists (e.g., Biomni, K-Dense) that are accelerating biological research, creating a need to systematically evaluate and mitigate these emerging biosecurity vulnerabilities.
AI.method: Paired evaluation framework using WMDP (Weapons of Mass Destruction proxies) benchmarks for biology and chemistry prompts; comparative testing of Biomni (agentic) vs. standalone model; introduction of BioVeil MATRIX — a defensive taxonomy with 10 tactical categories (TA01–TA10) and 22 techniques for mapping AI-enabled biosecurity risks.
AI.result: Agentic AI scientists bypass base model safeguards to assist with dual-use tasks; agentic scaffolding of Biomni increased benchmark performance (capability uplift) relative to the underlying standalone model on WMDP biology/chemistry prompts.
AI.conclusion: Additional safeguards must be added to existing models, and future tools should be built with agentic vulnerabilities in mind; BioVeil MATRIX provides a baseline taxonomy for developing specialized benchmarks and red-teaming protocols before public deployment.

title: CellxPert: Inference-Time MCMC Steering of a Multi-Omics Single-Cell Foundation Model for In-Silico Perturbation
summary: In this work, we introduce CellxPert, a scalable multimodal foundation model that unifies single-cell and spatial multi-omics within a common representation space. CellxPert jointly encodes transcriptomic (scRNA-seq), chromatin-accessibility (ATAC-seq), and surface-proteomic (CITE-seq) measurements, while directly incorporating MERFISH and imaging mass-cytometry data as 2D or 3D spatial-visual layers. CellxPert facilitates four key downstream tasks out of the box: (i) cell-type annotation across a broad ontology of 154 largely overlapping identities -- the largest label space addressed to date and a stringent test of fine-grained discrimination, (ii) efficient fine-tuning using Low Rank Adaptation (LoRA), (iii) genome-wide transcriptomic response prediction to in-silico perturbations (ISP), and (iv) seamless multi-omic integration across various assays and platforms. Unlike current single-cell foundation models, which approximate gene perturbations by deleting or reordering tokenized gene expression ranks, CellxPert employs a Metropolis-Hastings sampler whose proposal kernel uses the model's masked conditional distributions to transition to new transcriptomic states conditioned on the perturbed genes. This Markov-chain procedure mitigates out-of-distribution artifacts introduced by abrupt token manipulation and produces trajectories that are biologically interpretable. Evaluations on PBMC68K, Replogle Perturb-seq, Systema, and BMMC benchmarks show that CellxPert surpasses classical and state-of-the-art baselines in cell-type annotation, perturbation response prediction, and multi-omic integration.

AI.tldr: CellxPert is a multimodal foundation model that unifies single-cell and spatial multi-omics data, supporting four tasks: cell-type annotation across 154 labels, efficient LoRA fine-tuning, perturbation response prediction via Metropolis-Hastings sampling, and multi-omic integration. It outperforms baselines on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To create a scalable foundation model that jointly encodes multiple single-cell and spatial omics modalities (scRNA-seq, ATAC-seq, CITE-seq, MERFISH, imaging mass cytometry) in a common representation space, and to address limitations of existing models that use crude token manipulation for perturbation prediction.
AI.method: CellxPert uses a multimodal architecture with a shared representation space for transcriptomic, chromatin-accessibility, surface-proteomic, and spatial-visual data. For perturbation response prediction, it employs a Metropolis-Hastings sampler that uses masked conditional distributions as proposal kernels to generate biologically interpretable transcriptomic state transitions, avoiding out-of-distribution artifacts from abrupt token manipulation.
AI.result: CellxPert surpasses classical and state-of-the-art baselines on PBMC68K, Replogle Perturb-seq, Systema, and BMMC benchmarks for cell-type annotation, perturbation response prediction, and multi-omic integration. It supports the largest label space for cell-type annotation (154 largely overlapping identities).
AI.conclusion: CellxPert demonstrates that a multimodal foundation model with a principled Markov-chain Monte Carlo approach for perturbation modeling significantly outperforms existing methods, offering a scalable and biologically interpretable solution for single-cell and spatial multi-omics analysis.

title: Co-Generative De Novo Functional Protein Design
summary: De novo functional protein design aims to generate protein sequences that realize specified biochemical functions without relying on evolutionary templates, enabling broad applications in biotechnology and medicine. Existing approaches adopt either direct function-to-sequence mapping or decoupled structure-sequence generation strategies but often fail to achieve functionality and foldability simultaneously. To address this, we propose CodeFP, a Co-generative protein language model for de novo Functional Protein design that simultaneously decodes sequence and structure tokens, thereby enabling superior simultaneous realization of functionality and foldability. CodeFP utilizes functional local structures to enrich functional semantic encodings, overcoming the suboptimal translation of flat encodings into structure tokens, while introducing auxiliary functional supervision to alleviate training ambiguity stemming from the one-to-many structure-to-token mapping. Extensive experiments show that CodeFP consistently achieves average improvements of 6.1% in functional consistency and 3.2% in foldability over the strongest baseline.

AI.tldr: CodeFP is a co-generative protein language model that simultaneously decodes sequence and structure tokens for de novo functional protein design, achieving 6.1% better functional consistency and 3.2% better foldability than baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing de novo functional protein design methods (direct function-to-sequence mapping or decoupled structure-sequence generation) often fail to achieve both functionality and foldability simultaneously, motivating the need for a co-generative approach.
AI.method: CodeFP is a co-generative protein language model that simultaneously decodes sequence and structure tokens. It uses functional local structures to enrich functional semantic encodings and introduces auxiliary functional supervision to address training ambiguity from one-to-many structure-to-token mapping.
AI.result: CodeFP achieves average improvements of 6.1% in functional consistency and 3.2% in foldability over the strongest baseline across extensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: Simultaneous co-generation of sequence and structure tokens, enriched by functional local structures and auxiliary supervision, is an effective paradigm for de novo functional protein design that jointly optimizes both functionality and foldability.

title: Logistic Gene Regulatory Networks: Prevention of Expression Shutdown, and Numerical Stability Beyond Hill Function
comment: arXiv admin note: text overlap with arXiv:2512.14325
summary: Hill functions, the standard tool for modelling gene regulatory networks, carry three structural flaws when the cooperativity exponent is non-integer: loss of global smoothness, silent complex-valued arithmetic corruption of ODE trajectories, and an identically zero basal production rate that traps bistable models in off-states. Logistic functions $f^\pm$, being globally $C^\infty$, real-valued for all arguments, and strictly positive at zero, resolve all three simultaneously. For a two-gene negative-feedback oscillator, local asymptotic stability is established for all positive parameters via the Routh--Hurwitz criterion, and no Hopf bifurcation is possible without time delays. For bistable positive autoregulation, saddle-node thresholds are characterised through explicit transcendental equations; with biophysically grounded \textit{E.~coli} parameters, basal logistic production drives off-state escape in $\approx 44$~min while the Hill model remains permanently trapped. The 11-gene Traynard cell-cycle Boolean network is translated automatically via the product-of-logistics De~Morgan formalism and integrated without warnings, all variables remaining bounded and non-negative. The De~Morgan framework places every repressor threshold at a positive measurable concentration, whereas the weighted-sum formulation of Samuilik et al.\ places repressor critical points at negative concentrations, rendering them biologically inert. On an 80-gene Boolean-derived ODE system with $n = 3.509$, the Hill solver entered silent complex-valued contamination at $t \approx 52.64$ and terminated near $t \approx 63$--$65$; the logistic formulation completed $t \in [0, 200]$ without a single warning. The always-positive production rate ensures full controllability, enabling sliding mode, model predictive, and feedback-linearisation strategies where Hill-based formulations fail.

AI.tldr: Logistic functions replace Hill functions in gene regulatory network modeling to fix three critical flaws: non-integer exponents cause non-smoothness, complex-valued arithmetic, and zero basal activity trapping bistable systems. Logistic models are smooth, real-valued, positive at zero, enabling correct oscillation, bistability, and scalable Boolean-to-ODE translation without numerical failures.
AI.motivation: Hill functions with non-integer cooperativity exponents suffer from three structural flaws: loss of global smoothness, silent complex-valued arithmetic corruption of ODE trajectories, and identically zero basal production that traps bistable models in off-states permanently.
AI.method: Proposes logistic functions \(f^\pm\) as globally smooth (\(C^\infty\)), real-valued, and strictly positive at zero replacements. Applies to a two-gene oscillator (Routh-Hurwitz stability analysis), bistable positive autoregulation (transcendental saddle-node equations), and Boolean-to-ODE translation via product-of-logistics De Morgan formalism. Benchmarked on 11-gene and 80-gene systems.
AI.result: Logistic models eliminate complex-valued contamination, enable off-state escape (~44 min in E. coli vs. permanent trapping in Hill model), and complete simulations without warnings. The De Morgan framework yields biologically meaningful repressor thresholds, unlike weighted-sum formulations. Logistic models enable advanced control strategies where Hill models fail.
AI.conclusion: Logistic functions resolve all three Hill function flaws simultaneously, providing a superior, numerically robust, and biologically consistent framework for modeling gene regulatory networks.

title: Modelling the electrophysiological interactions between human pluripotent cell-derived cardiomyocite grafts and host ventricular tissue
comment: 23 pages, 10 figures, as submitted to PLOS Comp Biol
summary: Human pluripotent stem cell-derived cardiomyocytes (hPSC-CMs) are a promising therapy for regenerating myocardium after infarction, but their use is limited by graft-related arrhythmias that frequently occur shortly after transplantation. Experimental studies indicate that these arrhythmias can originate within the graft, which may act as an ectopic pacemaker, yet the mechanisms governing successful excitation of host tissue remain poorly understood. In particular, the role of electrical coupling at the graft-host interface is important, but difficult to measure directly or control. Computer modelling can help here. Here, we present a computational framework that enables systematic investigation of graft-host electrical interactions using a physiologically interpretable parameterisation. We model the graft-host interface as an internal boundary with a defined specific conductance, allowing direct control over coupling strength in units that correspond to measurable tissue properties. We formulate the governing equations and implement the computations using both finite-difference and finite-element discretisations in established cardiac modelling platforms. Using representative anatomical and physiological configurations, we demonstrate how variations in interface conductance influence the ability of spontaneous graft activity to initiate propagating excitation in host tissue. This framework provides a reproducible, mechanistically transparent tool for studying graft-related arrhythmogenesis and lays a foundation for evaluating strategies to mitigate arrhythmic risk in cardiac cell therapy.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a computational framework to study how electrical coupling at the graft-host interface affects arrhythmias after hPSC-CM transplantation, using physiologically interpretable parameters to simulate spontaneous graft activity triggering host tissue excitation.
AI.motivation: Graft-related arrhythmias shortly after hPSC-CM transplantation limit their therapeutic use, and the mechanisms of electrical coupling at the graft-host interface are poorly understood and difficult to measure or control experimentally.
AI.method: A computational framework modeling the graft-host interface as an internal boundary with defined specific conductance, implemented using finite-difference and finite-element discretizations in established cardiac modeling platforms.
AI.result: Demonstrated how variations in interface conductance influence the ability of spontaneous graft activity to initiate propagating excitation in host tissue.
AI.conclusion: Provides a reproducible, mechanistically transparent tool for studying graft-related arrhythmogenesis and lays a foundation for evaluating strategies to mitigate arrhythmic risk in cardiac cell therapy.

title: Heat-tree: Cross-platform software for interactive and embeddable phylogenetic tree visualization and editing
summary: Phylogenetic trees are the primary framework for conveying evolutionary relationships. While many tools exist for visualizing phylogenetic trees, most are limited to static graphics, require coding expertise, or are developed for a specific website and not easily reusable or extensible. To address these limitations, we developed heat-tree, a collection of software packages in JavaScript, R, and Python for interactive visualization, manipulation, and editing of phylogenetic trees and their associated metadata. Heat-tree allows for the creation of customizable, web-compatible tree visualizations that can be easily embedded in R Markdown, Jupyter Notebooks, and Quarto documents, as well as directly in HTML/JavaScript applications and websites. The package supports radial and rectangular tree layouts, automated translation of metadata values into visual encodings on the tree, interactive tree editing, and export capabilities for publication-quality figures. All visualization parameters are definable programmatically or interactively using the comprehensive graphical user interface included with each visualization. Heat-tree was designed to be a user-friendly software package for interactive tree viewing, manipulation, editing, and self-contained, embeddable visualization across software environments.

AI.tldr: Heat-tree is a multi-language (JavaScript, R, Python) software package for creating interactive, customizable, and embeddable phylogenetic tree visualizations with editing capabilities and publication-quality export.
AI.motivation: Existing phylogenetic tree visualization tools are limited to static graphics, require coding expertise, or are platform-specific and not easily reusable or extensible.
AI.method: Developed heat-tree as a collection of software packages in JavaScript, R, and Python supporting radial/rectangular layouts, automated metadata-to-visual encoding, interactive editing, GUI-based control, and embedding in R Markdown, Jupyter Notebooks, Quarto, and HTML/JavaScript.
AI.result: Creates customizable, web-compatible, interactive tree visualizations with publication-quality export capabilities that are self-contained and embeddable across multiple software environments.
AI.conclusion: Heat-tree provides a user-friendly, cross-platform solution for interactive phylogenetic tree visualization, manipulation, and editing that addresses key limitations of existing tools.

title: How Light Reshapes the Mind. An Active Inference Framework for the Cognitive and Emotional Effects of Indoor Lighting
summary: Indoor lighting affects cognition, affect, and behavioural regulation, but these effects are often treated as isolated findings rather than as parts of a unified process. This paper proposes an active inference account of shared indoor lighting in multi-user environments such as offices, classrooms, and libraries. It argues that lighting shapes behaviour through three distinct channels: illuminance modulates perceptual precision, correlated colour temperature modulates arousal relative to circadian optimum, and spectral composition biases behavioural disposition toward engagement or rest. The paper formalises this hypothesis through a proof-of-concept POMDP model of agents performing sustained reading over five hours, using both reading performance and eye-tracking observations. The model generates six falsifiable predictions, all confirmed across 20 Monte Carlo simulations.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an active inference model explaining how indoor lighting impacts cognition and behavior through three channels (illuminance, color temperature, spectral composition), validated via POMDP simulations of reading performance.
AI.motivation: Indoor lighting effects on cognition, affect, and behavioral regulation are often studied as isolated findings rather than as parts of a unified process. The authors aim to bridge this gap using active inference.
AI.method: A proof-of-concept POMDP model was built for agents performing sustained reading over 5 hours, incorporating reading performance and eye-tracking observations. Lighting shapes behavior via three channels: illuminance (perceptual precision), correlated color temperature (arousal vs. circadian optimum), and spectral composition (engagement vs. rest bias).
AI.result: Six falsifiable predictions were generated, all confirmed across 20 Monte Carlo simulations.
AI.conclusion: Indoor lighting effects on cognition and behavior can be unified under an active inference framework, offering a principled account for multi-user environments like offices and classrooms.

title: PhenotypeToGeneDownloaderR: automated multi-source retrieval and validation of phenotype-associated genes
summary: Identifying phenotype-associated genes is a common first step in polygenic risk score construction, enrichment testing, target prioritisation and variant interpretation, but relevant evidence is distributed across heterogeneous databases with different interfaces, formats and evidence models. Here, we present PhenotypeToGeneDownloaderR, a phenotype-guided R/Python pipeline for automated gene retrieval, harmonisation, symbol validation and cross-source summary analysis. Given a phenotype term, the pipeline queries integrated biological databases, standardises per-source outputs, combines gene lists, validates retrieved symbols against the NCBI human gene reference and generates summary tables and visualisations. Across 13 clinically relevant phenotypes and 13 databases, PhenotypeToGeneDownloaderR generated 136,487 raw gene retrievals, with at least one source returning genes for every phenotype. Across all 13 phenotypes, 100,175 of 114,345 combined input symbols were retained after direct or synonym-based validation, corresponding to an 87.6\% validation rate. Cross-source overlap was low, supporting the complementarity of integrated evidence sources. Against an HPO/ClinVar/OMIM-derived gold standard, the pipeline recovered 1,039 of 1,056 known phenotype-associated genes, corresponding to 98.4\% recall. PhenotypeToGeneDownloaderR provides a lightweight, reproducible upstream framework for generating candidate gene sets for downstream prioritisation and interpretation. The pipeline is implemented in R and Python, released under the MIT licence, and available at https://github.com/MuhammadMuneeb007/PhenotypeToGeneDownloaderR.

AI.tldr: PhenotypeToGeneDownloaderR is an R/Python pipeline that automatically retrieves, harmonizes, and validates phenotype-associated genes from 13 databases, achieving 87.6% symbol validation and 98.4% recall against a gold standard across 13 clinical phenotypes.
AI.motivation: Phenotype-associated gene evidence is scattered across heterogeneous databases with different interfaces, formats, and evidence models, making it difficult to aggregate for downstream tasks like polygenic risk scoring, enrichment testing, and variant interpretation.
AI.method: The pipeline queries 13 integrated biological databases given a phenotype term, standardizes outputs, combines gene lists, validates symbols against the NCBI human gene reference (direct and synonym-based), and generates summary tables and visualizations. Implemented in R and Python under MIT license.
AI.result: Across 13 phenotypes and 13 databases, 136,487 raw retrievals were generated. 100,175 of 114,345 combined symbols (87.6%) were validated. Cross-source overlap was low, confirming database complementarity. Against an HPO/ClinVar/OMIM gold standard, the pipeline recovered 1,039 of 1,056 genes (98.4% recall).
AI.conclusion: PhenotypeToGeneDownloaderR provides a lightweight, reproducible upstream framework for generating candidate gene sets that can serve as input for downstream prioritization and interpretation tasks.

title: Measuring Understanding Through Discrete Compositional Knowledge Structures in Hierarchical Automata
summary: How do we measure genuine understanding in artificial cognitive systems? Current approaches face a measurement gap: probabilistic systems refine confidence gradually, practice-based systems compile knowledge through repeated execution, and neural systems distribute understanding across opaque embedding spaces. We propose that making understanding measurable requires architectures where understanding formation produces discrete, inspectable structural signatures. This paper presents hierarchical automata built from finite state machines representing patterns and higher-order automata representing compositions. Constrained inference constructs automata from single observations. Similarity detection clusters related automata, making concept robustness quantifiable. Graph memory makes compositional knowledge directly inspectable. Metacognitive mechanisms enable observable reconfiguration. We demonstrate understanding measurement in a simple geometric domain. Graph evolution tracking reveals five measurable signatures: immediate representation formation, structural knowledge, generalization capacity, compositional awareness, and metacognitive access. These measurements distinguish structural understanding from statistical correlation. Our contribution is a framework for making understanding measurable through discrete compositional knowledge structures. This measurement capability complements perceptual learning in neural systems and task execution in neurosymbolic architectures.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using hierarchical automata (finite state machines + higher-order automata) to make understanding measurable through five discrete structural signatures: immediate representation formation, structural knowledge, generalization capacity, compositional awareness, and metacognitive access.
AI.motivation: Current AI systems (probabilistic, practice-based, neural) face a measurement gap where genuine understanding cannot be reliably distinguished from statistical correlation.
AI.method: Builds hierarchical automata via constrained inference from single observations; uses similarity detection to cluster related automata, graph memory for inspectable compositional knowledge, and metacognitive mechanisms for observable reconfiguration.
AI.result: Demonstrates in a simple geometric domain that five measurable signatures can distinguish structural understanding from statistical correlation.
AI.conclusion: The framework makes understanding measurable through discrete compositional knowledge structures, complementing neural and neurosymbolic approaches.

title: From Cortical Synchronous Rhythm to Brain Inspired Learning Mechanism: An Oscillatory Spiking Neural Network with Time-Delayed Coordination
summary: Human cognition emerges from coordinated spiking dynamics in distributed neural circuits, where information is encoded via both firing rates and precise spike timing determined by brain rhythms. Inspired by this notion, we propose a brain-inspired learning primitive in which cognition-level neural synchrony emerges through iterative bottom-up and top-down interactions between micro-scale dynamics of spiking neurons and a macro-scale mechanism of oscillatory synchronization. Specifically, we model each parcel (e.g., a cortical region or an image pixel) in the target system as a spiking neuron embedded in a predefined connectivity scaffold. Low-level information is encoded in a spatiotemporal domain, where neurons are selectively grouped and fire spontaneously over time through self-organized dynamics. In the bottom-up route, oscillatory synchronization is formed from past spiking activity accumulated over a finite memory window. Since brain dynamics operate in a regime of partial and transient synchronization rather than global phase locking, we model oscillatory coordination using a time-delayed synchronization formulation, which enables a top-down modulation of heterogeneous neural spiking for a large-scale distributed system. Together, we devise a spiking-by-synchronization neural network (S2-Net) that uses rhythmic timing as a control mechanism for efficient information processing. Promising results have been achieved across a broad range of tasks, including neural activity decoding, energy-efficient signal processing, temporal binding and semantic reasoning.

AI.tldr: S2-Net is a brain-inspired spiking neural network that uses oscillatory synchronization through bottom-up and top-down interactions between spiking neurons and rhythmic timing for efficient information processing across multiple tasks.
AI.motivation: To bridge micro-scale spiking neuron dynamics and macro-scale oscillatory synchronization, mimicking how human cognition emerges from coordinated spiking dynamics and brain rhythms.
AI.method: Models each cortical region or pixel as a spiking neuron with connectivity scaffold; uses bottom-up oscillatory synchronization from past spiking activity with finite memory, and top-down modulation via time-delayed synchronization for heterogeneous spiking coordination.
AI.result: Achieved promising results on neural activity decoding, energy-efficient signal processing, temporal binding, and semantic reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Spiking-by-synchronization framework with rhythmic timing control enables efficient and distributed neural information processing across diverse tasks.

title: Electroencephalography and Electromyography as a Non-Invasive Biomarker of Neural Regeneration: A Review of Central and Peripheral Nervous System Injury and Regeneration
summary:
Regeneration of the nervous system after injury remains an important therapeutic objective, especially in the central nervous system (CNS), in which regeneration is restricted by both neuronal limitations as well as adverse extracellular environments. Conversely, the peripheral nervous system (PNS) displays enhanced regenerative capability in the presence of supportive Schwann cells (SC) and pro-growth stimuli. While the structure and molecular mechanisms are thoroughly understood, functional biomarkers that can non-invasively monitor regeneration in real time are limited. In this review, we discuss the promise of electroencephalography (EEG) as well as electromyography (EMG) as real-time, non-invasive biomarkers to monitor damage to nerves and regeneration in both CNS and PNS contexts.
  First, we contrast biological and electrophysiological indicators of CNS/PNS injury, showing how EEG signs, including oscillatory power, connectivity, and evoked potential changes, reflect dysfunction due to injury as well as neuroplastic reorganization. Also, EMG provides direct insight into muscle activation and peripheral output, providing useful EEG complementation in neuromuscular pathway integrity and reactivation. In CNS injuries (e.g., stroke, spinal cord injury (SCI)), EEG typically shows global slowing, disrupted interhemispheric coherence, and partial recovery of higher frequencies. For PNS injuries, EEG can capture cortical remapping and return of somatosensory evoked responses with re-establishment of the peripheries' connectivity. EMG, in turn, enables monitoring of reinnervation and restoration of functional motor output.
  This review presents a dual-system perspective, positioning EEG and EMG not only as diagnostic tools but also as functional biomarkers of neural regeneration, thereby bridging electrophysiology, plasticity, and clinical recovery.

AI.tldr: This review advocates for EEG and EMG as non-invasive, real-time functional biomarkers to monitor nerve injury and regeneration in both CNS and PNS, highlighting how EEG captures neuroplastic changes and EMG tracks peripheral reinnervation, together bridging electrophysiology and clinical recovery.
AI.motivation: Nervous system regeneration remains a major therapeutic challenge, particularly in the CNS where regeneration is limited. While the molecular and structural mechanisms of regeneration are well understood, there is a lack of functional biomarkers that can non-invasively monitor regeneration in real time.
AI.method: The authors review and contrast biological and electrophysiological indicators of CNS/PNS injury, examining how EEG (oscillatory power, connectivity, evoked potentials) and EMG (muscle activation, peripheral output) can serve as complementary tools to monitor injury, neuroplastic reorganization, and regeneration across both CNS injuries (stroke, SCI) and PNS injuries.
AI.result: EEG in CNS injuries shows global slowing, disrupted interhemispheric coherence, and partial recovery of higher frequencies; in PNS injuries, EEG captures cortical remapping and return of somatosensory evoked responses. EMG enables monitoring of reinnervation and restoration of functional motor output.
AI.conclusion: EEG and EMG are positioned as dual-system functional biomarkers of neural regeneration — not merely diagnostic tools — bridging electrophysiology, plasticity, and clinical recovery for both CNS and PNS contexts.

title: Emergent population dynamics of random walkers with cooperative reproduction and spatial selection
summary: We extend the $N$ branching Brownian motions model of population invasion to higher-order asexual reproduction. Increasing reproduction order leads to qualitative changes: invasion fronts generically cease to exist beyond binary reproduction; and in the binary case itself, their speed becomes diffusion-independent. Ternary reproduction shows critical behavior, with collapse into a strongly localized `invasion bullet' in the supercritical regime, diffusive spreading in the subcritical regime, and a continuous family of fronts at criticality. These results suggest that the dominance of division and binary reproduction in nature reflects fundamental constraints on invasion dynamics.

AI.tldr: This paper extends branching Brownian motion models to higher-order asexual reproduction, finding that invasion fronts become qualitatively different beyond binary reproduction, with ternary reproduction exhibiting critical behavior and a collapse into a localized "invasion bullet" in the supercritical regime, suggesting that binary reproduction dominance in nature reflects fundamental dynamical constraints.
AI.motivation: To understand why binary reproduction (division) dominates in nature over higher-order asexual reproduction strategies, and to explore the fundamental constraints on invasion dynamics.
AI.method: The authors extend the N branching Brownian motions model of population invasion to higher-order asexual reproduction, analyzing different reproduction orders (binary, ternary, etc.) theoretically.
AI.result: Invasion fronts generically cease to exist beyond binary reproduction; binary reproduction speed becomes diffusion-independent; ternary reproduction shows critical behavior with collapse into a localized 'invasion bullet' in the supercritical regime, diffusive spreading in the subcritical regime, and a continuous family of fronts at criticality.
AI.conclusion: The dominance of division and binary reproduction in nature reflects fundamental constraints on invasion dynamics, as higher-order reproduction leads to qualitatively different and often non-front-like behaviors.

title: Longitudinal QSM: Enhancing consistency of multiple time point susceptibility maps via simultaneous reconstruction
summary: Quantitative susceptibility mapping (QSM) has been increasingly applied in longitudinal studies of neurodegenerative diseases and aging to assess temporal alterations in brain iron and myelin. The accuracy of such investigations depends on the repeatability and sensitivity of measurements. However, the ill-posed nature of the QSM processing steps makes the reconstruction vulnerable to background field changes, head orientation changes, noise, and imperfect registration, which compromise repeatability and sensitivity and hinder reliable detection of true changes. To address these limitations, we propose Longitudinal QSM, a simultaneous reconstruction framework that jointly estimates susceptibility maps across time points while enforcing spatial sparsity of temporal changes. The method was evaluated through simulations and in-vivo experiments and compared with conventional reconstruction methods. Longitudinal QSM consistently reduced inter-scan variability and accurately recovered simulated lesion changes. Application to stroke patient and multiple sclerosis patient data further demonstrated that the framework stabilizes non-lesion variability while preserving lesion-related temporal changes. This approach offers a promising tool for monitoring subtle temporal changes in brain iron and myelin in various neurodegenerative diseases as well as throughout aging and development.

AI.tldr: Longitudinal QSM is a simultaneous reconstruction framework that jointly estimates susceptibility maps across time points, reducing inter-scan variability while preserving true temporal changes, making it promising for monitoring brain iron and myelin changes in neurodegenerative diseases and aging.
AI.motivation: QSM suffers from poor repeatability and sensitivity due to ill-posed reconstruction issues (background field changes, head orientation, noise, imperfect registration), hindering reliable detection of true temporal changes in longitudinal studies of brain iron and myelin.
AI.method: The proposed Longitudinal QSM framework jointly estimates susceptibility maps across multiple time points simultaneously, enforcing spatial sparsity of temporal changes to stabilize reconstruction.
AI.result: The method consistently reduced inter-scan variability, accurately recovered simulated lesion changes, and in stroke/MS patient data, stabilized non-lesion variability while preserving lesion-related temporal changes.
AI.conclusion: Longitudinal QSM offers a promising tool for monitoring subtle temporal changes in brain iron and myelin across neurodegenerative diseases, aging, and development.

title: ORBIT: Learning Gene Program Co-Activation Structure for Cell-Type-Stratified Pathway Rewiring Analysis in Single-Cell Transcriptomics
summary: Gene programs co-activate within cells, but existing single-cell methods either treat programs independently or require experimental perturbation data to model their interactions. We introduce ORBIT, a self-supervised transformer that learns asymmetric dependencies among gene programs from observational single-cell RNA-sequencing data alone, quantifying how strongly each program influences every other program. The key mechanism is an intervention-consistent training objective: the model learns each program's directional influence on every other program by predicting how the others change when that program is removed, yielding attention weights that reflect asymmetric influence rather than symmetric co-occurrence. Applied to 191,890 prefrontal cortex nuclei across three pathway vocabularies, ORBIT recovers co-activation structure consistent with established Alzheimer's disease vulnerability signatures, identifies cell-type-specific rewiring invisible to differential expression, and achieves 0.984 macro F1 on cell-type classification from 220 pathway scores, which is within 0.3 points of a state-of-the-art classifier using all 22,088 genes.

AI.tldr: ORBIT is a self-supervised transformer that learns asymmetric, directional dependencies among gene programs from single-cell RNA-seq data alone, achieving strong cell-type classification and revealing disease-relevant program interactions.
AI.motivation: Existing methods treat gene programs independently or need perturbation data to model their interactions; the authors aim to learn directional program dependencies from purely observational data.
AI.method: A self-supervised transformer with an intervention-consistent training objective that predicts how other programs change when a target program is removed, producing attention weights reflecting asymmetric (directional) influence.
AI.result: Recovers co-activation structure matching Alzheimer's vulnerability signatures, identifies cell-type-specific rewiring invisible to differential expression, and achieves 0.984 macro F1 on cell-type classification using only 220 pathway scores (within 0.3 points of a 22,088-gene classifier).
AI.conclusion: ORBIT effectively models asymmetric gene program interactions from observational data, enables biological discovery, and achieves near state-of-the-art classification with far fewer features.

title: Inferring Active Neural Circuits Using Diffusion Scores
summary: In biological systems, neural circuits compute through directed, short-latency interactions whose effects unfold across multiple time scales and behavioral contexts. We address the problem of inferring these local, lag-specific interactions from sampled neural population activity under varying stimuli, without assuming a parametric form for the underlying dynamics. Our approach leverages denoising score models by estimating joint-window scores over consecutive activity snapshots (i.e., brain states) and converting these scores into calibrated, directed edge tests via cross-block score products. The key insight is that these products recover the Jacobian of the transition map between brain states under nonlinear dynamics. To cleanly separate lag-specific effects, we introduce minimal multi-block windows that condition on intermediate time points, avoiding the omitted-lag bias inherent in pairwise analyses. The resulting method, Score--Block Time Graphs (SBTG), identifies lag-specific directed interactions in sampled neuronal population data. We specifically apply SBTG to whole-brain C. elegans calcium imaging data to recover lag-specific circuit structure not resolved by current methods, including improved alignment with independent connectomes, cell-type-specific temporal organization, and neuromodulatory profiles consistent with known receptor kinetics. These findings highlight the potential for SBTG to serve as a practical ``AI for science'' tool by turning high-dimensional neural population recordings into statistically testable circuit hypotheses.

AI.tldr: SBTG infers lag-specific directed neural interactions from population activity using score-based Jacobian estimation, demonstrated on C. elegans data to recover circuit structure beyond existing methods.
AI.motivation: Biological neural circuits compute through directed, short-latency interactions across multiple time scales and behavioral contexts, but existing methods struggle to infer these local, lag-specific interactions from sampled neural data without assuming parametric dynamics.
AI.method: Uses denoising score models to estimate joint-window scores over consecutive brain state snapshots, converts scores into directed edge tests via cross-block score products (which recover the Jacobian of the transition map under nonlinear dynamics), and introduces minimal multi-block windows conditioning on intermediate time points to avoid omitted-lag bias.
AI.result: Applied to whole-brain C. elegans calcium imaging data, SBTG recovers lag-specific circuit structure not resolved by current methods, showing improved alignment with connectomes, cell-type-specific temporal organization, and neuromodulatory profiles matching known receptor kinetics.
AI.conclusion: SBTG serves as a practical "AI for science" tool that transforms high-dimensional neural recordings into statistically testable circuit hypotheses.

title: Foundation Model Guided Dual-Branch Co-Adaptation for Source-Free EEG Decoding
summary: Source-free domain adaptation (SFDA) provides a practical solution to cross-subject EEG decoding by adapting source-pretrained models to unlabeled target domains without accessing source data. However, existing SFDA methods rely solely on the limited internal knowledge of source-pretrained models, leading to inferior cross-domain generalization and unreliable pseudo-labels. Although EEG Foundation Models (FMs) pretrained on large-scale data exhibit strong generalizability, their potential in SFDA remains largely unexplored. To this end, we propose FUSED, a Foundation-guided Source-free EEG Decoding framework that integrates a large-scale FM with a compact Specialist Model (SM) via dual-branch co-adaptation. Specifically, we introduce a Co-adaptation mechanism equipping both branches with linear and prototype views, enabling cross-branch pseudo-label generation. Additionally, we design a Consensus Filtering Mechanism that exploits the FM's inherent stability to identify high-quality samples, along with a Two-Stage Pseudo-Label Refinement scheme to suppress error accumulation through cross-branch arbitration. Finally, we calibrate the FM's decision boundaries via mutual information maximization with the SM, followed by knowledge distillation from FM to SM, forming a principled calibrate-then-distill pipeline. To our knowledge, FUSED is the first work to leverage EEG FMs within the SFDA framework for cross-subject EEG decoding. Extensive experiments across three EEG paradigms, including motor imagery, emotion recognition, and SSVEP, demonstrate consistent state-of-the-art performance, validating the effectiveness of foundation-guided synergy for robust and privacy-preserving EEG decoding.

AI.tldr: FUSED is a framework that integrates a large-scale EEG Foundation Model with a compact Specialist Model via dual-branch co-adaptation for source-free domain adaptation in cross-subject EEG decoding, achieving state-of-the-art results across three EEG paradigms.
AI.motivation: Existing SFDA methods rely solely on limited internal knowledge of source-pretrained models, leading to poor cross-domain generalization and unreliable pseudo-labels. Although EEG Foundation Models show strong generalizability, their potential in SFDA remains unexplored.
AI.method: FUSED introduces: (1) a dual-branch co-adaptation mechanism with linear and prototype views for cross-branch pseudo-label generation; (2) a Consensus Filtering Mechanism using FM's stability to identify high-quality samples; (3) a Two-Stage Pseudo-Label Refinement scheme to suppress error accumulation; (4) mutual information maximization for decision boundary calibration, followed by knowledge distillation from FM to SM.
AI.result: Consistent state-of-the-art performance across three EEG paradigms: motor imagery, emotion recognition, and SSVEP, demonstrating effectiveness of foundation-guided synergy for robust and privacy-preserving EEG decoding.
AI.conclusion: FUSED is the first work to leverage EEG Foundation Models within the SFDA framework, validating that foundation-guided synergy significantly improves cross-subject EEG decoding performance.

title: How Well Can We Decode Vowels from Auditory EEG -- A Rigorous Cross-Subject Benchmark with Honest Assessment
comment: 31 pages, 11 figures; includes supplementary material (14 pages, additional figures and analyses)
summary: EEG based phoneme decoding is promising for brain computer interfaces, but many prior studies rely on within subject evaluation, small cohorts, or weak leakage control. We present a reproducible cross subject benchmark for five class vowel decoding (a, e, i, o, u) from auditory EEG using OpenNeuro ds006104 (16 subjects, 61 channels, 256 Hz). Under strict leave one subject out evaluation with training only normalization and explicit anti leakage checks, we compare 14 pipelines from classical machine learning, deep learning, and Riemannian methods. The best full feature model (XGBoost) reaches 24.5 percent accuracy (chance 20 percent), while differential entropy features with LightGBM reach 25.5 percent in feature specific analysis. After multiple comparison correction, strong pairwise model advantages are limited. Classical methods are competitive with deep models in this low signal regime. Additional analyses (ablation, pairwise vowels, within subject CV, ERP, temporal generalization, and electrode importance) indicate that vowel information is real but weak and mainly carried by early transient auditory responses. We release code and evaluation scripts for full reproducibility.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a rigorous cross-subject benchmark for decoding five vowels from EEG, showing that vowel information is real but weak (~25% accuracy vs 20% chance), with classical ML methods performing competitively with deep learning under strict leave-one-subject-out evaluation.
AI.motivation: Prior EEG-based phoneme decoding studies often suffer from within-subject evaluation, small sample sizes, and weak leakage controls, making results unreliable. The authors aim to establish a reproducible, strictly evaluated benchmark.
AI.method: 14 pipelines (classical ML, deep learning, Riemannian methods) are compared on OpenNeuro ds006104 (16 subjects, 61 channels, 256 Hz) using strict leave-one-subject-out evaluation with anti-leakage checks. Differential entropy features and XGBoost/LightGBM are tested.
AI.result: Best accuracy is 25.5% (chance 20%) using differential entropy + LightGBM. Classical methods match deep learning performance. Vowel information is weak and mainly from early transient auditory responses.
AI.conclusion: Cross-subject vowel decoding from EEG is feasible but limited; classical ML remains competitive in low SNR settings. Code and scripts are released for reproducibility.

title: Linking spatial biology and clinical histology via Haiku
summary: Integrating molecular, morphological, and clinical data is essential for basic and translational biomedical research, yet systematic frameworks for jointly modeling these modalities remain limited. Here we present Haiku, a tri-modal contrastive learning model trained on multiplexed immunofluorescence (mIF). It comprises 26.7 million spatial proteomics patches from 3,218 tissue sections across 1,606 patients spanning 11 organ types, with matched hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) histology and clinical metadata aligned in a shared embedding space. Haiku enables three-way cross-modal retrieval, improves downstream classification and clinical prediction tasks over unimodal baselines, and supports zero-shot biomarker inference through fusion retrieval conditioned on clinical metadata-only text descriptions. Across tasks, Haiku outperforms competing approaches, achieving cross-modal retrieval (Recall@50 up to 0.611 versus near-zero baseline), survival prediction (C-index 0.737, +7.91% relative improvement), and zero-shot biomarker inference (mean Pearson correlation 0.718 across 52 biomarkers). Furthermore, we introduce a counterfactual prediction framework in which modifying only clinical metadata while fixing tissue morphology surfaces niche-specific molecular shifts associated with breast cancer stage progression and lung cancer survival outcomes. In a lung adenocarcinoma case study, the counterfactual analysis recovers niche-specific shifts characterized by increased CD8 and granzyme B, reduced PD-L1, and decreased Ki67, broadly consistent with patterns reported for favorable outcomes. We present these counterfactual results as exploratory, hypothesis-generating signals rather than mechanistic claims. These capabilities demonstrate that tri-modal alignment via Haiku enables integrative analysis of spatial biology, bridging molecular measurements with clinical context for biological exploration.

AI.tldr: Haiku is a tri-modal contrastive learning model integrating mIF, H&E histology, and clinical metadata from over 1,600 patients, enabling cross-modal retrieval, improved survival prediction (C-index 0.737), zero-shot biomarker inference (mean Pearson r=0.718), and counterfactual analysis revealing niche-specific molecular shifts in cancer progression.
AI.motivation: Despite the importance of integrating molecular, morphological, and clinical data for biomedical research, there are limited systematic frameworks that jointly model these modalities in a unified manner.
AI.method: Haiku is a tri-modal contrastive learning model trained on 26.7 million spatial proteomics patches from mIF, with matched H&E histology and clinical metadata aligned in a shared embedding space.
AI.result: Haiku achieves cross-modal retrieval Recall@50 up to 0.611 (vs near-zero baseline), survival prediction C-index 0.737 (+7.91% improvement), and zero-shot biomarker inference mean Pearson r=0.718 across 52 biomarkers.
AI.conclusion: Tri-modal alignment via Haiku enables integrative analysis of spatial biology, bridging molecular measurements with clinical context for biological exploration and hypothesis generation.

title: Robust volatility updates for Hierarchical Gaussian Filtering
summary: Hierarchical Gaussian Filtering (HGF) networks allow for efficient updating of posterior distributions (beliefs) about hidden states of an agent's environment. HGF parent nodes can target the mean or variance of their children. New information entering at input nodes leads to a cascade of belief updates across the network according to one-step update equations for each node's mean and precision (inverse variance). However, the original form of the update equations for variance-targeting parents(volatility coupling) can in some regions of parameter space lead to negative posterior precision, a logical impossibility which causes the updating algorithm to terminate with an error. In this report, we introduce a modified quadratic approximation to the variational energy of volatility-coupled nodes that avoids negative posterior precision. The key idea is to interpolate between two quadratic expansions of the variational energy: one at the prior prediction and one at a second mode whose location is obtained in closed form via the Lambert W function. The resulting update equations are robust across the entire parameter space and faithfully track the variational posterior even for large prediction errors.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses a critical flaw in Hierarchical Gaussian Filtering (HGF) for volatility-coupled nodes, where posterior precision can become negative and cause algorithmic failure. The authors propose a robust quadratic approximation using Lambert W function interpolation that prevents negative precision and maintains accuracy across all parameter spaces.
AI.motivation: The original HGF update equations for variance-targeting parents (volatility coupling) can produce negative posterior precision in certain parameter regions, which is logically impossible and causes the updating algorithm to crash. A robust fix is needed.
AI.method: The authors introduce a modified quadratic approximation to the variational energy of volatility-coupled nodes by interpolating between two quadratic expansions: one at the prior prediction and one at a second mode whose location is derived in closed form using the Lambert W function.
AI.result: The proposed update equations are robust across the entire parameter space and faithfully track the variational posterior even when large prediction errors occur.
AI.conclusion: The modified approximation successfully resolves the negative precision problem without sacrificing accuracy, making HGF volatility coupling reliable across all parameter regimes.

title: Label-Free Microrefractometry of Interfacial Processes Using Fluorescent Smart Coverslips
comment: 38 pages, 4 figures (main text) and 9 supporting figures
summary: Molecular dipoles near interfaces emit highly directional radiation due to near-field interactions, making surface-bound fluorophores sensitive probes of local physicochemical changes. We introduce smart coverslips, stably coated with uniform, brightly fluorescent nanobead films, that exploit refractive-index-dependent emission shifts for sensitive micro-refractometry in small volumes. Supercritical-angle fluorescence refractometry uses single back-focal-plane images to allow us real-time RI sensing and nanometric thin-film height measurements without the need for multi-angle or multi-wavelength acquisition. Our fast, label-free, and non-invasive approach allows measurements of thin-film properties and monitoring of interfacial dynamics on a standard inverted microscope and is broadly applicable to nanobiophotonics, chemical sensing, and in-situ materials analysis.

AI.tldr: A smart coverslip coated with fluorescent nanobead films enables real-time refractive index sensing and thin-film height measurements through supercritical-angle fluorescence refractometry using single back-focal-plane images, offering label-free, non-invasive interfacial analysis on standard microscopes.
AI.motivation: To develop a sensitive, label-free, and non-invasive micro-refractometry method that exploits directional emission from molecular dipoles near interfaces for monitoring local physicochemical changes in small volumes.
AI.method: Smart coverslips coated with uniform, brightly fluorescent nanobead films; supercritical-angle fluorescence refractometry using single back-focal-plane imaging to detect refractive-index-dependent emission shifts.
AI.result: Real-time RI sensing and nanometric thin-film height measurements achieved without multi-angle or multi-wavelength acquisition, using a standard inverted microscope.
AI.conclusion: The approach is broadly applicable to nanobiophotonics, chemical sensing, and in-situ materials analysis, providing fast and non-invasive interfacial monitoring.

title: Computational foundations of the human world
summary: Human societies continuously transform scattered information into collective judgments and coordinated action, whether through markets discovering prices, governments allocating resources, communities enforcing norms, or science converging on reliable claims. Importantly, the computational difficulty of collective decision-making, particularly the time and communication required to reach solutions, imposes fundamental constraints on social organization. While theoretical computer science offers formal tools for analyzing such problems, for instance, by analyzing resource requirements, including time and memory, surprisingly, there is no domain of social science that focuses on the nature of computation in the human world. This perspective argues that we now have the opportunity to deploy these computational frameworks to study human social organization, opening research directions at the intersection of computer science and social science. We highlight core social phenomena that can be framed as computational, including (i) distributed consensus and coordinated action, (ii) societal restructuring with scale, (iii) hierarchical and modular structure, and (iv) externalized memory systems. We identify several concepts from theoretical computer science that may provide insight into these phenomena, especially emphasizing more recently developed approaches beyond the paradigm of Turing~Machines and worst-case computational complexity.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that theoretical computer science frameworks can be deployed to study human social organization, highlighting how collective decision-making (distributed consensus, societal scaling, hierarchical structures, externalized memory) can be analyzed computationally, and calls for new interdisciplinary approaches beyond Turing Machines and worst-case complexity.
AI.motivation: The computational difficulty of collective decision-making—particularly time and communication costs—imposes fundamental constraints on social organization, yet no social science domain explicitly focuses on the nature of computation in human societies. The authors see an opportunity to bridge this gap.
AI.method: Conceptual/argumentative review: the authors identify core social phenomena (distributed consensus, societal scaling, hierarchy, externalized memory) and map them to concepts from theoretical computer science, while advocating for frameworks beyond Turing Machines and worst-case complexity.
AI.result: Four key phenomena are framed as computational problems: (i) distributed consensus and coordinated action, (ii) societal restructuring with scale, (iii) hierarchical and modular structure, (iv) externalized memory systems. The paper calls for newer theoretical CS approaches to analyze these.
AI.conclusion: There is a promising opportunity to apply computational frameworks—especially modern theoretical CS tools—to study human social organization, opening new research directions at the intersection of computer science and social science.

title: Benchmarking Single-Pose Docking, Consensus Rescoring, and Supervised ML on the LIT-PCBA Library: A Critical Evaluation of DiffDock, AutoDock-GPU, GNINA, and DiffDock-NMDN
summary:
Virtual screening performance depends heavily on the chosen docking and scoring methods. Recent AI-based tools such as DiffDock and NMDN have reported strong benchmark results, but their practical utility on realistic, experimentally-derived datasets remains unclear. Here we perform a large-scale evaluation on the LIT-PCBA library (15 targets, 578,295 ligand-target pairs with experimentally confirmed actives and inactives). We compare AutoDock-GPU and DiffDock for pose generation, followed by rescoring with GNINA and NMDN. We further evaluate rank-based consensus strategies and supervised machine learning models trained on docking features.
  GNINA rescoring of AutoDock-GPU poses (AutoDock-GNINA) emerged as the strongest single method with a median EF1% of 2.14. DiffDock-based approaches underperformed relative to AutoDock-GNINA, particularly on challenging targets such as OPRK1. Carefully designed consensus ranking improved robustness but did not surpass the best single scorer. Supervised ML re-ranking delivered the largest gains, achieving a median EF1% of 4.49 (+110% over AutoDock-GNINA).
  Our results highlight that even the best classical+ML hybrid workflows provide only modest early enrichment on realistic benchmarks. We conclude that no single docking method dominates across targets and that rigorously validated, cost-effective combinations with supervised re-ranking currently offer the most practical value for virtual screening.

AI.tldr: A large-scale evaluation on LIT-PCBA (15 targets, ~578K pairs) shows AutoDock-GPU + GNINA rescoring outperforms DiffDock approaches, and supervised ML re-ranking boosts median EF1% by 110% to 4.49, though early enrichment remains modest overall.
AI.motivation: To assess whether recent AI-based docking tools (DiffDock, NMDN) outperform classical methods on realistic, experimentally-derived screening datasets, given that benchmark results may not translate to practical utility.
AI.method: Evaluated AutoDock-GPU vs DiffDock for pose generation, rescored with GNINA and NMDN, and tested consensus strategies and supervised ML models on LIT-PCBA (15 targets, 578,295 ligand-target pairs).
AI.result: AutoDock-GNINA achieved median EF1% of 2.14; DiffDock-based methods underperformed; consensus improved robustness but not peak performance; supervised ML re-ranking achieved median EF1% of 4.49 (+110% over best single method).
AI.conclusion: No single docking method dominates all targets; cost-effective classical+ML hybrid workflows with supervised re-ranking currently offer the most practical value, though early enrichment remains modest even with the best approaches.

title: Statistics of a multi-factor function from its Fourier transform
comment: Submitted to the Journal of Fourier Analysis and Applications. 42 pages, 6 figures
summary: For a phenomenon $\pmb{f}$ that is a function of $\mathit{n}$ factors, defined on a finite abelian group $\mathcal{G}$, we derive its population statistics solely from its Fourier transform $\hat{\pmb{f}}$. Our main result is an $\mathit{m-Coefficient/Index Annihilation Theorem}$: the $\mathit{m}$th moment of $\pmb{f}$ becomes a series of terms, each with precisely $\mathit{m}$ Fourier coefficients -- and surprisingly, the coefficient $\mathit{indices}$ in each term sum to zero under group addition. This condition acts like a filter, limiting which terms appear in the Fourier domain, and can reveal deeper relationships between the variables driving $\pmb{f}$. These techniques can also be used as an analytical/design tool, or as a feasibility constraint in search algorithms. For functions defined on $\mathbb{Z}_2^n$, we show how the skew, kurtosis, etc. of a binomial distribution can be derived from the Fourier domain. Several other examples are presented.

AI.tldr: This paper derives population statistics (moments) of functions on finite abelian groups directly from their Fourier transforms, with a key result being the m-Coefficient/Index Annihilation Theorem that filters Fourier terms based on index sums equaling zero.
AI.motivation: To establish a direct connection between Fourier-domain representations and statistical moments of functions defined on finite abelian groups, enabling analysis without explicit domain computation.
AI.method: The authors prove an m-Coefficient/Index Annihilation Theorem showing that the mth moment of a function decomposes into terms containing exactly m Fourier coefficients, where coefficient indices must sum to zero under group addition — acting as a filter that constrains which terms contribute.
AI.result: The theorem reveals that moment terms are limited by an index-sum-zero condition. Applied to functions on ℤ₂ⁿ, the authors demonstrate how skewness, kurtosis, and other moments of binomial distributions can be derived from the Fourier domain.
AI.conclusion: The Fourier-domain moment derivation provides an analytical/design tool and feasibility constraint for search algorithms, with examples showing broad applicability beyond the binomial case.

title: Modeling sequential cognitive states via population level cortical dynamics
summary: In this work, we present a mathematical model for cyclic and sequential patterns of brain activity, combining heteroclinic dynamics with discrete neural-field models. We first show that spatial-discrete neural-field equations with biologically realistic equilibria cannot support heteroclinic cycles. On the other hand, heterocline dynamics often arise in Lotka-Volterra-type systems, but these equations do not directly correspond to neuronal processes. To address this, we use a version of the Universal Approximation Theorem to approximate any target dynamics by a neural network interpretable as a high-dimensional Amari-type neural-field system. When the target dynamics contains a heteroclinic cycle, the approximating vector field generates a periodic trajectory that closely follows the heteroclinic connection. As a case study, we consider the cognitive processes underlying focused-attention meditation. We show how the model reproduces sequential transitions among cognitive states and we conclude providing a neural interpretation of the approximating dynamics.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a mathematical model linking heteroclinic dynamics and neural-field equations to explain sequential brain activity patterns, using the Universal Approximation Theorem to approximate heteroclinic cycles with neural networks. A case study on focused-attention meditation demonstrates how the model reproduces sequential cognitive state transitions.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between two separate mathematical frameworks—heteroclinic dynamics (common in Lotka-Volterra systems) and neural-field models—that individually cannot capture sequential brain activity patterns, in order to provide a biologically plausible model of cyclic and sequential cognitive processes.
AI.method: The authors prove that spatial-discrete neural-field equations with biologically realistic equilibria cannot support heteroclinic cycles. They then leverage the Universal Approximation Theorem to approximate target dynamics (including heteroclinic cycles) using a neural network that can be interpreted as a high-dimensional Amari-type neural-field system, producing periodic trajectories that follow heteroclinic connections.
AI.result: The approximating vector field successfully generates periodic trajectories that closely follow heteroclinic cycles. The model reproduces sequential transitions among cognitive states in the focused-attention meditation case study.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach successfully combines heteroclinic dynamics with neural-field models through neural network approximation, offering a neural interpretation of sequential cognitive processes and providing a mathematical framework for understanding brain activity patterns underlying meditation and potentially other cognitive tasks.

title: Genealogical structures under interactive neutral reproduction: factorial moment duality via a Frankenstein process
summary: We establish a genealogical framework for an existing analytical moment duality between a Wright--Fisher type SDE and a counting process with interaction. To achieve this, we construct a finite-population Moran model featuring interactive neutral reproduction as a novel mechanism. In the corresponding events, an individual, regardless of its own type, can only reproduce if a randomly encountered partner is of the ``fit'' type. This Moran model has a relatively simple counting process as its factorial moment dual, whose genealogical meaning appears to be cryptic: after all, the line-counting process of the natural genealogical process of the model, namely the ancestral influence graph (AIG), exhibits a complex hierarchical structure not reflected in the factorial moment dual. Since moment duality is a property in expectation, we are allowed to systematically remove information from the AIG and merge different realizations of the ancestry. We call the result the \emph{Frankenstein process}. Based on this, we establish the factorial moment duality from a genealogical perspective. The moment duality in the diffusion limit follows in a natural way.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a genealogical framework for the moment duality between a Wright-Fisher type SDE and a counting process with interaction. The authors introduce a Moran model with interactive neutral reproduction and construct the "Frankenstein process" by systematically removing information from the ancestral influence graph to explain the factorial moment duality from a genealogical perspective.
AI.motivation: To establish a genealogical (probabilistic) understanding of an existing analytical moment duality between a Wright-Fisher type SDE and a counting process with interaction, whose genealogical meaning was previously cryptic.
AI.method: The authors construct a finite-population Moran model with interactive neutral reproduction (where an individual can only reproduce if a randomly encountered partner is of the "fit" type). They then systematically remove information from the ancestral influence graph (AIG) by merging different realizations of ancestry, creating the "Frankenstein process" to derive the factorial moment duality from a genealogical perspective.
AI.result: The factorial moment duality is established from a genealogical perspective via the Frankenstein process, and the moment duality in the diffusion limit follows naturally.
AI.conclusion: The genealogical framework successfully explains the moment duality that previously appeared cryptic, showing that information removal from the AIG yields the dual process. The diffusion limit confirms the connection to the original analytical duality.

title: Online Generalised Predictive Coding
summary: This paper introduces an extension of generalised filtering for online applications. Generalised filtering refers to data assimilation schemes that jointly infer latent states, learn unknown model parameters, and estimate uncertainty in an integrated framework -- e.g., estimate state and observation noise -- at the same time (i.e., triple estimation). This framework appears across disciplines under different names, including variational Kalman-Bucy filtering in engineering, generalised predictive coding in neuroscience, and Dynamic Expectation Maximisation (DEM) in time-series analysis. Here, we specialise DEM for ``online'' data assimilation, through a separation of temporal scales. We describe the variational principles and procedures that allow one to assimilate data in a way that allows for a slow updating of parameters and precisions, which contextualise fast Bayesian belief updating about the dynamic hidden states. Using numerical studies, we demonstrate the validity of online DEM (ODEM) using a non-linear -- and potentially chaotic -- generative model, to show that the ODEM scheme can track the latent states of the generative process, even when its functional form differs fundamentally from the dynamics of the generative model. Framed from a neuro-mimetic predictive coding perspective, ODEM offers a biologically inspired solution to online inference, learning, and uncertainty estimation in dynamic environments.

AI.tldr: This paper extends Dynamic Expectation Maximisation (DEM) for online data assimilation by separating temporal scales, enabling slow parameter/precision updates alongside fast Bayesian belief updating of hidden states, validated on nonlinear chaotic models.
AI.motivation: To adapt generalised filtering (triple estimation of states, parameters, and noise) for online applications where data arrives sequentially, moving beyond batch processing methods.
AI.method: Specialises DEM for online assimilation by introducing a temporal scale separation: fast Bayesian belief updating for latent states and slow updating for parameters/precisions, presented from a neuro-mimetic predictive coding perspective.
AI.result: Online DEM (ODEM) successfully tracks latent states of a nonlinear/chaotic generative process, even when the generative model's dynamics differ fundamentally from the true process.
AI.conclusion: ODEM provides a biologically inspired framework for online inference, learning, and uncertainty estimation in dynamic environments, bridging neuroscience and engineering.

title: Bolek: A Multimodal Language Model for Molecular Reasoning
summary:
Molecular property models increasingly support high-stakes drug-discovery decisions, but their outputs are often difficult to audit: classical predictors return scores without rationale, while language models can produce fluent explanations weakly grounded in the input molecule.
  We introduce Bolek, a compact multimodal language model that grounds natural-language reasoning in molecular structure by injecting a Morgan fingerprint embedding into an instruction-tuned text decoder. Bolek is fine-tuned on molecular alignment tasks, including molecule description, RDKit descriptor prediction, and substructure detection, and on downstream reasoning over 15 TDC binary classification tasks using synthetic chains-of-thought anchored in concrete molecular features.
  Across these tasks, Bolek outperforms its Qwen3-4B-Instruct base on all endpoints in yes/no mode and on 13 of 15 in chain-of-thought mode, raising mean ROC/PR AUC from 0.55 to 0.76. It also outperforms TxGemma-9B-Chat on 13 of 15 binary classification tasks despite being less than half its size. Bolek's explanations are more grounded than those of the baseline LLMs: it cites numerical descriptors 10-100x more often per chain-of-thought, and the cited values agree strongly with RDKit for key descriptors such as TPSA, MolLogP, and MolWt (Spearman rho = 0.87-0.91). Generalisation extends beyond the training panel: on 15 unseen TDC classification endpoints, Bolek matches TxGemma on five, and it produces non-trivial rank correlations on three held-out regression endpoints despite never seeing downstream regression during training.
  These results suggest that targeted modality injection and reasoning supervision tied to verifiable molecular features can yield compact, auditable molecular reasoning models.

AI.tldr: Bolek is a compact multimodal language model that grounds reasoning in molecular structure via Morgan fingerprint injection, outperforming larger models on 15 binary classification tasks while producing more verifiable and auditable explanations.
AI.motivation: Molecular property models support drug-discovery decisions but their outputs are hard to audit: classical predictors give scores without rationale, and language models produce fluent but weakly grounded explanations. There is a need for models that provide both accurate predictions and auditable, structure-grounded reasoning.
AI.method: Bolek injects a Morgan fingerprint embedding into an instruction-tuned text decoder (Qwen3-4B-Instruct), fine-tuned on molecular alignment tasks (description, RDKit descriptor prediction, substructure detection) and 15 TDC binary classification tasks using synthetic chains-of-thought anchored in concrete molecular features.
AI.result: Bolek outperforms Qwen3-4B-Instruct base on all endpoints in yes/no mode and 13/15 in CoT mode, raising mean ROC/PR AUC from 0.55 to 0.76. It outperforms TxGemma-9B-Chat on 13/15 tasks despite being half the size. Its explanations cite numerical descriptors 10-100x more often, with strong agreement to RDKit (Spearman rho=0.87-0.91). It generalizes to unseen tasks, matching TxGemma on 5/15 unseen classification endpoints and producing non-trivial rank correlations on held-out regression tasks.
AI.conclusion: Targeted modality injection and reasoning supervision tied to verifiable molecular features can yield compact, auditable molecular reasoning models that are both accurate and explainable.
